questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a file system in an operating system?
questions.option_b: B. To organize, store, and retrieve data on storage devices
questions.option_c: C. To allocate memory to running processes
questions.option_d: D. To encrypt all user data automatically
questions.explanation: A file system manages how data is stored, organized, and retrieved from storage devices.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a file in an operating system?
questions.option_b: B. A collection of related information stored on secondary storage
questions.option_c: C. A process currently executing in RAM
questions.option_d: D. A hardware component used for storage
questions.explanation: A file is the smallest unit of logical storage containing related information on secondary storage.

questions.question: Which file attribute uniquely identifies a file internally within the operating system?
questions.explanation: The identifier is the unique internal ID used by the operating system to distinguish files.

questions.question: What is the role of a directory in a file system?
questions.option_c: C. Organize and store information about files
questions.explanation: Directories organize files logically and store metadata for efficient searching and management.

questions.question: Which operation moves the current position within a file?
questions.explanation: The seek operation changes the file pointer position within a file.

questions.question: Why does an operating system maintain an open-file table?
questions.option_a: A. To permanently store deleted files
questions.option_b: B. To improve performance and manage open files efficiently
questions.explanation: The open-file table stores information such as file pointers and access rights to improve efficiency.

questions.question: Which file access method is most suitable for reading a log file line by line?
questions.explanation: Sequential access is efficient when data is processed in order from beginning to end.

questions.question: Which access method allows a program to directly access record number 100 in a file?
questions.explanation: Direct access allows random access to any location within a file.

questions.question: Which operation is NOT typically performed on a directory?
questions.explanation: Directories manage files and metadata, not CPU instruction execution.

questions.question: What is a major disadvantage of a single-level directory structure?
questions.explanation: Single-level directories create naming conflicts because all users share the same directory.

questions.question: What advantage does a two-level directory provide?
questions.option_a: A. Eliminates the need for path names
questions.option_c: C. Allows different users to have files with the same name
questions.option_d: D. Prevents all access control issues
questions.explanation: Two-level directories separate user directories, allowing identical file names for different users.

questions.question: Which type of directory structure is most hierarchical and commonly used in modern systems?
questions.explanation: Tree-structured directories support hierarchical organization of files and directories.

questions.question: Which file permission allows a user to modify file contents?
questions.explanation: Write permission allows modification or addition of data in a file.

questions.question: In UNIX permissions, what does the numeric permission 7 represent?
questions.explanation: Permission 7 corresponds to RWX (111 in binary).

questions.question: Which UNIX command attaches a group to a file?
questions.explanation: The chgrp command changes the group ownership of a file.

questions.question: Which layer of the file-system structure interacts directly with hardware devices?
questions.explanation: Device drivers communicate directly with storage hardware.

questions.question: What information is typically stored in a File Control Block (FCB)?
questions.option_c: C. File permissions, size, and dates
questions.explanation: An FCB stores metadata such as inode number, permissions, size, and timestamps.

questions.question: Which file allocation method stores each file in contiguous disk blocks?
questions.explanation: Contiguous allocation stores files in sequential neighboring blocks.

questions.question: What is a major problem associated with contiguous allocation?
questions.option_c: C. Inability to perform direct access
questions.explanation: Contiguous allocation suffers from external fragmentation as files grow and shrink.

questions.question: In contiguous allocation, how many I/O operations are needed to access the 8th block directly?
questions.explanation: Contiguous allocation supports direct access with only one I/O operation.

questions.question: What is an extent in extent-based file systems?
questions.option_a: A. A random collection of memory addresses
questions.option_b: B. A contiguous block of disk space
questions.explanation: An extent is a contiguous sequence of disk blocks used for file allocation.

questions.question: Which statement about linked allocation is TRUE?
questions.option_a: A. It requires all blocks to be contiguous
questions.option_b: B. It supports efficient direct access
questions.option_c: C. Each block contains a pointer to the next block
questions.option_d: D. It completely eliminates internal fragmentation
questions.explanation: Linked allocation stores pointers linking each block to the next block.

questions.question: Why is linked allocation inefficient for direct access?
questions.option_b: B. Blocks must be traversed sequentially
questions.option_c: C. It cannot store large files
questions.explanation: To access a specific block, all previous blocks must be traversed.

questions.question: A file uses linked allocation and contains 10 blocks. How many I/O operations are needed to access the 6th block?
questions.explanation: Linked allocation requires traversal through the first six blocks.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of indexed allocation?
questions.explanation: Indexed allocation enables efficient direct access through index blocks.

questions.question: What is a disadvantage of indexed allocation?
questions.option_c: C. Extra storage overhead for index blocks
questions.explanation: Indexed allocation requires additional storage space for index blocks.

questions.question: A file uses indexed allocation. If the index block is already in memory, how many I/O operations are needed to access a data block?
questions.explanation: Only the target data block must be accessed when the index block is cached.

questions.question: A file contains 10 blocks using indexed allocation. How many I/O operations are needed to read the entire file?
questions.explanation: One I/O reads the index block and ten more read the data blocks.

questions.question: Which modern file system is commonly associated with Linux?
questions.explanation: ext4 is a widely used Linux file system.

questions.question: What mechanism improves file system performance by reducing repeated disk access?
questions.explanation: Buffer caches store frequently accessed data in memory to improve efficiency.

questions.question: A database system frequently accesses random records in huge files. Which access method is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Direct access supports fast random retrieval of records.

questions.question: A university server stores files for thousands of users. Which directory structure is MOST suitable?
questions.explanation: Tree-structured directories support scalable hierarchical organization.

questions.question: Which allocation method provides the fastest direct block access in most cases?
questions.explanation: Contiguous allocation calculates physical addresses directly.

questions.question: A file is expected to grow frequently and unpredictably. Which allocation method is LEAST suitable?
questions.explanation: Contiguous allocation struggles with file growth due to external fragmentation.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why linked allocation avoids external fragmentation?
questions.option_a: A. Blocks can be scattered anywhere on the disk
questions.option_d: D. Files use only one block
questions.explanation: Linked allocation allows blocks to be stored non-contiguously.

questions.question: A system administrator wants both efficient direct access and minimal traversal overhead. Which allocation method should be selected?
questions.explanation: Indexed allocation supports direct access without traversing linked blocks.

questions.question: Which scenario best demonstrates sequential access?
questions.option_a: A. Streaming a video file from beginning to end
questions.option_c: C. Jumping directly to the last database entry
questions.option_d: D. Editing a random byte in memory
questions.explanation: Sequential access processes data in order from start to finish.

questions.question: Why are protection mechanisms important in multi-user operating systems?
questions.option_c: C. To prevent unauthorized access to files
questions.explanation: Protection mechanisms enforce secure access control among users.

questions.question: In UNIX permissions, what does the value 6 represent?
questions.explanation: Permission 6 corresponds to read and write permissions.

questions.question: A file contains 12 blocks using contiguous allocation. How many I/O operations are needed to read the entire file?
questions.explanation: Each block requires one read operation, totaling 12 I/Os.

questions.question: A file contains 8 blocks using linked allocation. How many I/O operations are needed to read the entire file?
questions.explanation: Each block must be accessed sequentially, requiring 8 I/Os.

questions.question: A file contains 10 blocks. Which allocation method requires exactly 2 I/O operations to access the 7th block?
questions.explanation: Indexed allocation requires one I/O for the index block and one for the target block.

questions.question: Which allocation method is MOST vulnerable to pointer corruption issues?
questions.explanation: Linked allocation depends heavily on block pointers; corruption can break file chains.

questions.question: Why do modern operating systems prefer extent-based systems over pure contiguous allocation?
questions.option_a: A. Extents eliminate all storage overhead
questions.option_b: B. Extents support flexible file growth while maintaining performance
questions.option_c: C. Extents remove the need for directories
questions.explanation: Extent-based systems combine performance benefits with better scalability and flexibility.

questions.question: Which component stores the current read/write location for an opened file?
questions.explanation: The file pointer tracks the current position within an open file.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the file-open count in an open-file table?
questions.option_b: B. Track how many processes currently opened the file
questions.option_c: C. Count the number of blocks in the file
questions.explanation: The file-open count tracks how many processes are using the file.

questions.question: A company needs extremely fast sequential reading of very large multimedia files. Which allocation method is MOST appropriate?
questions.explanation: Contiguous allocation provides excellent sequential performance due to adjacent block storage.

questions.question: Which allocation method introduces additional storage overhead because of pointers inside blocks?
questions.explanation: Linked allocation stores pointers in each block, reducing usable space.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST explains the purpose of caching mechanisms in file systems?
questions.option_a: A. To permanently replace hard disks
questions.option_b: B. To reduce disk access latency
questions.explanation: Caching stores frequently used data in faster memory to reduce disk access times.

questions.question: A student accidentally gives public users execute-only permission on a script. Which numeric permission corresponds to execute-only?
questions.explanation: Execute-only permission corresponds to binary 001, which equals 1.

questions.question: Which file-system layer is responsible for managing metadata and directory structures?
questions.explanation: The logical file system manages metadata, directories, and protection.

questions.question: A file system designer wants to minimize external fragmentation while maintaining reasonable direct access. Which method is the BEST compromise?
questions.explanation: Indexed allocation avoids external fragmentation while supporting efficient direct access.
